tool_definitions.name: grep_code
tool_definitions.description: Search source files using a text pattern.

path.description: Directory or file path to search.

tool_definitions.name: read_file
tool_definitions.description: Read the full contents of a file.

message.content: You are a code review assistant. Analyze the provided repository for security and correctness issues. Be precise: cite file paths and line numbers when referencing code.
What changed in the auth flow? I'm seeing unexpected token expiry errors in staging that don't reproduce locally.
I'll inspect the token refresh path and the session hydration logic to identify the expiry discrepancy.

steps.reasoning_content: The user sees expiry errors in staging but not locally. Two likely causes: (1) the TOKEN_TTL environment variable is set differently in staging, or (2) the validation logic has a boundary condition — e.g., a grace period of zero that fails on the exact expiry millisecond. I should check both the middleware validation code and the environment configuration.

results.content:
auth/middleware.py:42: # refresh token validation happens before session hydration
auth/middleware.py:67:     if not token.is_valid(): raise TokenExpiredError()

message.content: The grep results indicate validation occurs before session hydration. Let me read the middleware in full to understand the token validation sequence.

Found the root cause. The middleware calls validate_token with grace_period=0, meaning tokens are rejected the instant they expire — there is no buffer for in-flight requests. Combined with a short TOKEN_TTL in staging (to be confirmed), any request that arrives within the final milliseconds of a token's lifetime will fail.

Recommendation: add a 30–60 second grace period in the validate_token call, or switch to sliding expiry on each successful request to push the window forward.

steps.reasoning_content: grace_period=0 is the immediate cause. The TOKEN_TTL default is 900 seconds locally, but if staging overrides it to something much shorter (e.g. 300 seconds), boundary failures become far more frequent. I should confirm the staging value before writing up the final diagnosis.

message.content: Can you check whether staging has TOKEN_TTL set to something different?
Checking the staging environment configuration now.

results.content: infra/staging/env.yaml:8: TOKEN_TTL: "300"  # 5 minutes — deliberately short for staging

message.content:
Confirmed. Staging sets TOKEN_TTL=300 (5 minutes) versus the 900-second production default — three times shorter. With grace_period=0 in the middleware, any request arriving at the moment a token expires is rejected with no buffer. At a 5-minute TTL this boundary is hit far more often than at 15 minutes, which explains why the failures are visible in staging but not locally.

Immediate fix: change line 46 of auth/middleware.py to pass grace_period=30. Longer-term: consider sliding expiry so active sessions never expire mid-use.

steps.reasoning_content: The diagnosis is now complete. TOKEN_TTL=300 in staging + grace_period=0 in middleware = frequent boundary failures. The fix is straightforward. I should present both a surgical short-term fix (grace period) and a structural long-term alternative (sliding expiry).
